detail: getIntelSources() reads response.data['sources'] (orbguard_api_client.dart:1455). SourcesHandler.List returns {"data": sources, "total": len} (sources.go:53). The key never matches, so getIntelSources() always returns [] and intelligence_sources_screen.dart:60 always shows the 'No Intelligence Sources' empty state, hiding the real DB/DefaultSources() data.
fix: Change getIntelSources() to read response.data['data'] (or change sources.go to emit {"sources":[...]}). Pick one and make it consistent with the /intel/sources alias route the client hits (router.go:147).
domain: Threat Hunting / Actors / Campaigns / Intelligence / Sources / MITRE

title: Threat graph tab is dead: client calls /graph/nodes and /graph/relations which don't exist
detail: getGraphNodes()/getGraphRelations() GET /graph/nodes and /graph/relations. The /graph router only registers /correlation/{id}, /related/{id}, /temporal/{id}, /shared-infrastructure, /detect-campaigns, /ttp-similarity, /traverse, /search, /relationship, /sync, /build-relationships, /stats — there is no /nodes or /relations route. Both calls 404; the error is swallowed in ThreatHuntingProvider.loadGraphData (threat_hunting_provider.dart:328-331), so the Graph tab (threat_hunting_screen.dart:1252) always shows 'No threat graph data available' and the 'Load Graph Data' button never works.
fix: Add GET /graph/nodes and /graph/relations handlers in graph.go returning {"nodes":[...]} / {"relations":[...]} (e.g. backed by Graph.SearchGraph / GetStats over Neo4j or Postgres), OR point the Dart client at the existing /graph/search (POST) and /graph/related endpoints and adapt the parsing. Do not leave the calls hitting non-existent routes.
domain: Threat Hunting / Actors / Campaigns / Intelligence / Sources / MITRE

title: Correlation tab dead: GET /correlation has no handler (backend only exposes POST endpoints)
detail: getCorrelationResults() does GET /correlation and reads response.data['results']. The /correlation router only registers POST '/', POST '/batch', POST '/analyze', GET '/indicator/{id}*', POST '/campaigns/match', GET '/campaigns/detect', POST '/cluster', GET '/stats'. There is no GET at the /correlation root, so the request returns 404/405. Error swallowed in loadCorrelationRules (threat_hunting_provider.dart:347), leaving the Correlation tab permanently empty.
fix: Either add a GET /correlation handler that returns recent correlation results as {"results":[...]}, or change the Dart client to POST to /correlation with a body and parse the actual Correlate response shape. Align the response key ('results') with whatever the handler emits.
domain: Threat Hunting / Actors / Campaigns / Intelligence / Sources / MITRE

title: ML Models tab dead: GET /ml/models returns stats object, client expects {'models':[...]}
detail: getMLModels() GETs /ml/models and reads response.data['models']. Router maps ml.Get('/models') to ML.GetStats (router.go:392), which returns h.service.GetStats() — a stats map, not a {"models":[...]} list. So getMLModels() always returns [] and the ML tab (threat_hunting_screen.dart _buildMLContent) is empty.
fix: Add a dedicated ML.GetModels handler returning {"models":[...]} (model name, type, version, accuracy, trained_at) and route ml.Get('/models') to it; move stats to the existing /ml/stats route. Or change the Dart client to call /ml/stats and render stats instead of a models list.
domain: Threat Hunting / Actors / Campaigns / Intelligence / Sources / MITRE

title: Threat-hunting rule engine stubs: hash/domain/network/path checks always return empty
detail: executeHunt dispatches rules by type (_executeRule :593). Only RuleType.permission (_checkPermissions) and the NO_LAUNCHER_ICON branch of _checkBehaviors do real work via MethodChannel. _checkNetwork (:687), _checkHashes (:697), _checkDomains (:707), _checkPaths (:716) all return an empty list with placeholder comments ('In production, this would...', 'Would compare APK hashes...'). Consequently the Malware IOC Sweep (hash+domain rules), Data Exfiltration network rules, Network Anomaly hunt, and C2 domain detection produce zero findings even on a compromised device — a core anti-spyware feature returns fake 'clean' results.
fix: Implement the rule checks against real data: hash checks should call the backend /intelligence/check (or /check/batch) with installed-APK SHA256s; domain checks should resolve DNS history and call /intelligence/check or /url/check; network checks should pull connection/flow data via MethodChannel and flag beaconing/unusual ports. Wire findings to the existing OrbGuardApiClient so on-device hunts are enriched by the threat-intel backend instead of returning empty.
domain: Threat Hunting / Actors / Campaigns / Intelligence / Sources / MITRE

title: Investigation cases have no persistence (in-memory only, lost on restart)
location: APP lib/services/security/threat_hunting_service.dart:262 (_cases map) + createCase :774-801; no backend route in LAB router.go
detail: createCase() stores cases in a process-local Map<String,InvestigationCase> (_cases). getCases() reads only from that map. There is no save/load to disk or backend, and no /cases route exists in the LAB router. Every case (and all hunt results) is lost when the app restarts, and cases never sync across devices. The Cases tab (threat_hunting_screen.dart:681) is effectively volatile scratch state.
fix: Persist cases locally (e.g. sqflite/Hive) and/or add backend CRUD endpoints (POST/GET /cases, PATCH /cases/{id}) with a repository, then load existing cases in ThreatHuntingProvider.initialize().
domain: Threat Hunting / Actors / Campaigns / Intelligence / Sources / MITRE

title: Intelligence Sources: Add-Source dialog and enable/disable Switch are no-ops
detail: The per-source enable/disable Switch only mutates a local IntelSource.isEnabled field (setState) with no API call — toggling a feed does nothing server-side and resets on reload. The 'Add Intelligence Source' dialog collects a name + feed URL but the 'Add' button just calls Navigator.pop with no persistence. The backend only exposes GET /sources and GET /sources/{slug} (router.go:120-123) — there is no create/update endpoint, so these controls are structurally dead.
fix: Add backend POST /sources (create) and PATCH /sources/{slug} (enable/disable) endpoints + repository writes, expose corresponding client methods, and wire the Switch.onChanged and Add button to call them. Until backend support exists, disable/remove the controls rather than presenting fake interactivity.
domain: Threat Hunting / Actors / Campaigns / Intelligence / Sources / MITRE

title: Threat report endpoint does not persist (TODO: store report)
detail: Intelligence.Report validates the request then only logs it ('// TODO: Store report in database') and returns {"success":true,"message":"Report received and queued for review"}. No row is written to any reports table and nothing is queued — the success response is misleading. Any user-submitted threat indicator is silently dropped.
fix: Insert the report into a community_reports/threat_reports table via a repository (status=pending) so it can actually be reviewed; only return success after a successful write, otherwise return 500.
domain: Threat Hunting / Actors / Campaigns / Intelligence / Sources / MITRE

title: Community indicators endpoint hardcoded empty
detail: ListCommunity ignores the DB entirely and always returns {data: [], total: 0}. It never queries h.repos, so the 'community-reported indicators' feature returns nothing regardless of data, and (combined with the non-persisting Report handler above) the entire community-reporting loop is non-functional.
fix: Query a community/reported indicators repository (filtered by source=community) with the given limit/offset and return real rows, mirroring List()/ListPegasus().
domain: Threat Hunting / Actors / Campaigns / Intelligence / Sources / MITRE

title: Mobile sync ignores platform filter and returns empty indicator sets
detail: ListMobile drops the platform query param ('// TODO: Filter by platform' :131). MobileSync reads version/platform/full but '_ = platform // TODO: Use in query' (:170) and then always populates the response with EMPTY slices: full sync sets Indicators = [] (:183), delta sync sets New/Updated/RemovedIDs = [] (:185-188). So the optimized mobile sync endpoint never returns any indicators — the on-device IOC database is never populated from this path.
fix: Implement MobileSync to load indicators from the repository (full set when lastVersion==0/full=true, delta of changed/removed since lastVersion otherwise) filtered by the platform param, and set HasMore/version correctly. Apply the platform filter in ListMobile as well.
domain: Threat Hunting / Actors / Campaigns / Intelligence / Sources / MITRE

title: Graph 'fit/center' control and intelligence-core detail buttons are no-op
detail: The graph tab's target/center IconButton has an empty onPressed: () {} (no-op). In intelligence_core_screen the indicator-detail dialog has two action buttons at :692 and :705 with empty onPressed: () {} that do nothing when tapped.
fix: Implement the graph fit/re-center action (reset node layout/zoom) and wire the intelligence-core detail action buttons to real behavior (e.g. block/report/whitelist via the API), or remove them to avoid presenting non-functional controls.
domain: Threat Hunting / Actors / Campaigns / Intelligence / Sources / MITRE

title: Device security status endpoint path mismatch — 404 on every call
detail: The app requests GET /api/v1/device/security/status. The backend only registers GET /api/v1/device/{device_id}/security-status (handler GetSecurityStatus, device_security.go:654). chi treats 'security' as the {device_id} param and there is no '/security/status' sub-route, so the request 404s. DeviceSecurityProvider.loadStatus() (device_security_provider.dart:224-242) catches the failure in a bare `catch (e) { // Use default status }` and keeps the default DeviceSecurityStatus(isLost:false, securityScore:100). The Status tab therefore ALWAYS shows 'Device Secure / 100' regardless of real backend state — fake data presented as real.
fix: Make the client build the parameterized path using the registered device_id, e.g. GET /api/v1/device/$deviceId/security-status, and pass _deviceId from the provider into the API method. Align all device methods to the {device_id} route shape, or add backend aliases. Also stop swallowing errors — surface a load error state to the UI instead of silently defaulting to score 100.
domain: Device Security / Permissions / Access Setup (OrbGuard APP + LAB)

title: Locate device path + HTTP verb mismatch
detail: App does GET /api/v1/device/locate; backend registers POST /api/v1/device/{device_id}/locate (handler Locate, device_security.go:248). Wrong path AND wrong method. Even if routed, the backend Locate handler only issues a CommandLocate remote command and returns {status:'locate_requested', command_id} — it never returns latitude/longitude. The provider (device_security_provider.dart:299-306) reads data['latitude']/['longitude'] which would throw on a successful response too. The Locate button and Location tab can never display a real location.
fix: Client must POST /api/v1/device/$deviceId/locate. Backend locate flow must be completed: device agent reports its GPS via POST /device/{device_id}/location (UpdateLocation exists, router.go:463), and the locate result must be fetched/streamed back. Provider should read the returned location coordinates from a real persisted location, not assume the command response carries lat/lng.
domain: Device Security / Permissions / Access Setup (OrbGuard APP + LAB)

title: Anti-theft settings GET/PUT path mismatch
detail: App calls GET/PUT /api/v1/device/anti-theft/settings; backend registers GET/PUT /api/v1/device/{device_id}/settings (GetSettings/UpdateSettings). All settings load/save 404. loadSettings() swallows the error and keeps defaults; updateSettings() optimistically mutates UI then fails silently (device_security_provider.dart:267-288). Toggling Remote Locate/Lock/Wipe/SIM-monitoring etc. never persists. Additionally the JSON keys differ: client sends locate_enabled/lock_enabled/wipe_enabled (lines 274-281) but the model expects enable_remote_locate/enable_remote_lock/enable_remote_wipe (models/device_security.go:191-193), so even on the right path no field would bind.
fix: Use path /device/$deviceId/settings and map field names to the backend model (enable_remote_locate, enable_remote_lock, enable_remote_wipe, enable_thief_selfie, enable_sim_alert, selfie_after_attempts, etc.). Add a round-trip integration test asserting persisted values come back.
domain: Device Security / Permissions / Access Setup (OrbGuard APP + LAB)

title: Send device command (lock/wipe/ring) path mismatch
detail: App posts to /api/v1/device/command/lock (and /command/wipe, /command/ring). Backend has POST /device/{device_id}/lock, /wipe, /ring, and a generic /device/{device_id}/command (IssueCommand). None match /device/command/{name}, so Lock/Wipe/Ring buttons all 404. _sendCommand (device_security_provider.dart:353-371) returns false and sets _error but the buttons just stop spinning — anti-theft actions are entirely non-functional. Wipe payload key also mismatches: client sends confirmation_code (device_security_provider.dart:340) but backend WipeCommandPayload requires confirmation_id (models/device_security.go:114, validated handlers/device_security.go:314).
fix: Map commands to dedicated endpoints: lock->/device/$deviceId/lock, wipe->/device/$deviceId/wipe, ring->/device/$deviceId/ring; or POST the generic /device/$deviceId/command with {type:...}. Rename wipe field to confirmation_id and generate a real confirmation id rather than the literal string 'WIPE'.
domain: Device Security / Permissions / Access Setup (OrbGuard APP + LAB)

title: Mark lost/stolen/recovered path mismatch
detail: App posts /api/v1/device/status/lost etc.; backend registers /api/v1/device/{device_id}/mark-lost etc. All 404. markAsLost/Stolen/Recovered (device_security_provider.dart:374-437) optimistically flip local UI state inside the try BUT only after the await — actually they call the API first then set state, so on 404 the catch sets _error and the status never changes. The 'Mark Lost/Stolen' and 'Mark as Recovered' controls are non-functional against the server.
fix: Use /device/$deviceId/mark-lost, /mark-stolen, /mark-recovered. Confirm server state via a follow-up GetSecurityStatus.
domain: Device Security / Permissions / Access Setup (OrbGuard APP + LAB)

title: Location history & SIM history & trusted-SIM & audit paths mismatch + response key mismatch
detail: getLocationHistory -> GET /device/location/history (backend: /device/{device_id}/location/history, returns {locations:...}; client reads response.data['locations'] OK but path wrong -> 404). getSIMHistory -> GET /device/sim/history (backend /device/{device_id}/sim/history returns key 'events' not 'sim_history'; client reads response.data['sim_history'] line 273 so even on correct path it gets []). addTrustedSIM -> POST /device/sim/trusted with {sim_id,name} (backend /device/{device_id}/sim/trusted expects {iccid}, handlers/device_security.go:439-445 -> 'iccid is required'). auditOSVulnerabilities -> POST /device/security/audit-os with no body (backend POST /device/vulnerabilities/audit requires {platform,os_version}, handlers:621 -> 400). All four are broken; Location/SIM tabs and the 'Scan' vulnerabilities button never return data.
fix: Fix paths to the {device_id} shape (/device/$deviceId/location/history, /sim/history, /sim/trusted) and /device/vulnerabilities/audit. Change SIM history parser to read 'events'. Send {iccid:...} for trusted SIM. Send {device_id,platform,os_version,security_patch,api_level} for the OS audit.
domain: Device Security / Permissions / Access Setup (OrbGuard APP + LAB)

title: Backend device-security state is in-memory only — not persisted, lost on restart
location: LAB internal/domain/services/device_security.go:33-62 (maps: devices, commands, simInfo, simEvents, selfies, settings, locations)
detail: Locations, SIM info/events, remote commands, thief selfies are stored only in Go maps (comment line 35 'Ephemeral stores, not persisted to DB'). Only the device record and settings are cached in Redis (RegisterDevice:83-106). On process restart all location history, SIM change events, pending commands and selfies vanish. For an anti-theft product this means a stolen device's location history / pending wipe command is not durable. GetLocationHistory/GetSIMHistory/GetThiefSelfies all read from these volatile maps.
fix: Back these stores with PostgreSQL (the db tags already exist on the models, e.g. RemoteCommand/SIMInfo/ThiefSelfie). Add a DeviceSecurityRepository with persistence for locations, sim_events, commands and selfies, and load pending commands from DB so GetPendingCommands survives restarts.
domain: Device Security / Permissions / Access Setup (OrbGuard APP + LAB)

title: No on-device agent reports location, SIM, or selfie data, and no command polling
location: APP lib/providers/device_security_provider.dart (entire) — no calls to UpdateLocation/ReportSIM/RecordThiefSelfie/GetPendingCommands; LAB router.go:454,463,467,473 expose these but app never calls them
detail: The backend models a device-agent protocol: device POSTs its location (/location), SIMs (/sim), selfies (/selfie) and polls /commands/pending then ACKs /commands/{id}/ack. The Flutter app implements NONE of this — there is no background service that reports GPS, reads SIM/ICCID (no telephony plugin in pubspec — geolocator/location/telephony absent), captures a thief selfie, or polls for and executes pending lock/wipe/ring commands. So even with paths fixed, 'Locate' has no source location, SIM tab is always empty, thief-selfie toggle does nothing, and a remote Lock/Wipe command issued from another device would never be executed locally. The whole anti-theft loop is non-functional client-side.
fix: Implement a device-agent: add geolocator + a telephony/SIM plugin + camera; on app start and via background/push, report location & SIM to /device/$id/location and /sim; implement a pending-command poller (or FCM push) that executes lock/ring/wipe and posts /commands/$id/ack; capture selfie on failed unlock and POST /selfie. Wire provider.locateDevice() to read the reported location.
domain: Device Security / Permissions / Access Setup (OrbGuard APP + LAB)

title: Device Security screen & provider are never wired into the app (unreachable, provider not registered)
location: APP lib/screens/device_security/device_security_screen.dart (only self-reference); lib/providers/device_security_provider.dart not in MultiProvider list (lib/main.dart:114-130)
detail: DeviceSecurityScreen is not navigated to from anywhere (grep shows only its own file references it) and DeviceSecurityProvider is NOT registered in the ChangeNotifierProvider list in main.dart (other providers are). The screen does context.read<DeviceSecurityProvider>() in initState (device_security_screen.dart:25) — if it were ever pushed it would throw ProviderNotFoundException. So the entire Device Security feature (Status/Anti-Theft/Location/SIM tabs) is dead/unreachable in the shipping app.
fix: Register ChangeNotifierProvider(create: (_) => DeviceSecurityProvider()) in main.dart and add a navigation entry (menu/dashboard tile) that pushes DeviceSecurityScreen. Verify the provider resolves.
domain: Device Security / Permissions / Access Setup (OrbGuard APP + LAB)

title: Dead buttons in Root/Jailbreak instruction screens and Thief-selfie 'View'
location: APP lib/screens/root_instructions_screen.dart:73-75 (Visit XDA, empty onPressed) & 81-85 ('Test Root Access' just pops); lib/screens/jailbreak_instructions_screen.dart:112-115 ('Test Jailbreak Status' just pops); lib/screens/device_security/device_security_screen.dart:524-527 ('View' photos onPressed:{})
detail: RootInstructionsScreen 'Visit XDA Developers Forum' has an empty onPressed (no url_launcher). 'Test Root Access' / 'Test Jailbreak Status' buttons claim to test access but only Navigator.pop — they never invoke checkRootAccess/checkJailbreak nor show a result. The Captured Photos 'View' button in DeviceSecurityScreen has onPressed: () {} (no-op) so thief selfies can never be viewed.
fix: Wire 'Visit XDA' to url_launcher; make Test buttons call MethodChannel('com.orb.guard/system').invokeMethod('checkRootAccess') and display the result; implement the selfie 'View' to open a gallery of provider.thiefSelfies image URLs.
domain: Device Security / Permissions / Access Setup (OrbGuard APP + LAB)

title: Advanced detection MethodChannel methods unimplemented on both Android and iOS
detail: grep of android MainActivity.kt and ios AppDelegate.swift shows zero handlers for any of these method names. BehavioralAnomalyDetector reads battery drain/CPU/network/screen-on/bg-process/data-usage all via invokeMethod which will throw MissingPluginException and each getter returns 0.0 (catch blocks lines 127,136,145,154,163,172). So behavioral anomaly scoring runs on all-zero inputs — it can never flag a behavioral anomaly. Several RootingMalwareDetector and KeystrokeLoggerDetector probes likewise return [] because their native methods don't exist. The 'behavioral' and parts of 'keylogger'/'rooting' advanced modules are effectively no-ops presented as active scans (main.dart:427-462 runs them).
fix: Implement the missing native handlers in MainActivity.kt (UsageStatsManager for usage/data, BatteryManager, /proc for CPU, getEnabledInputMethodList for IME) and in AppDelegate.swift where feasible (or have the Dart side skip unsupported methods on iOS). Until implemented, mark these modules as unavailable rather than returning fabricated 0/empty results that silently pass the scan.
domain: Device Security / Permissions / Access Setup (OrbGuard APP + LAB)

title: Device register response key mismatch (token vs api_key) and DeviceSecurityProvider duplicate registration
location: APP lib/services/api/orbguard_api_client.dart:108-116 (reads data['token']) & lib/providers/device_security_provider.dart:213-216 (registerDevice -> reads result['device_id']) vs LAB internal/api/handlers/auth.go:216-222 (returns 'api_key', 'device_id')
detail: AuthHandler.RegisterDevice returns {device_id, api_key, expires_at}. The API client's _registerDevice (line 109) reads data['token'] which is null, so the API key is never saved to the auth interceptor — subsequent authenticated /device/* calls send no/blank credential (X-Device-ID only). Separately, DeviceSecurityProvider._registerDevice posts {} to authDevice (device_security_provider.dart:215) which fails the backend's required device_id check (auth.go:162) and falls back to a fabricated 'local-...' id, so the device is never the same id the backend knows.
fix: In _registerDevice read data['api_key'] (not 'token') and persist via saveTokens. Have DeviceSecurityProvider reuse the already-registered device_id from the auth interceptor instead of re-registering with an empty body / generating a local id.
domain: Device Security / Permissions / Access Setup (OrbGuard APP + LAB)

title: No native handler for WiFi method channel — all device WiFi scanning dead on every platform
detail: NetworkProvider and RogueAPProvider invoke 'getCurrentNetwork', 'scanNetworks', and 'scanWifiNetworks' on MethodChannel('com.orb.guard/wifi'), but no Android/iOS/macOS/Windows/Linux native code registers this channel (grep over all platform dirs finds only com.orb.guard/system, /sms, /browser, /permissions). Every call throws MissingPluginException, caught and reduced to an error string or debugPrint, so the Network Security WiFi tab and Rogue AP 'Nearby' tab always show zero networks while the scan button animates as if working.
fix: Implement the 'com.orb.guard/wifi' MethodChannel natively: Android via WifiManager.startScan()/getScanResults + ConnectivityManager (with ACCESS_FINE_LOCATION runtime permission), iOS via NEHotspotNetwork.fetchCurrent (current network only; document that nearby scan is impossible on iOS and adapt the UI), and register the handlers in MainActivity.kt/AppDelegate.swift. Unsupported platforms should return a typed 'unsupported' result the UI surfaces honestly instead of an eternal empty list.

title: WiFi audit request/response models completely mismatch backend — audit always analyzes nothing and parses nothing
detail: The app sends a flat body {ssid, bssid, security_type, signal_strength, ...} but the Go WiFiAuditRequest expects {current_network: {...}, nearby_networks: [...], gateway_ip, dns_ip}. JSON decode succeeds with CurrentNetwork=nil and NearbyNetworks=nil, so services/network_security.go AuditWiFi runs all checks on nothing and always returns a 'safe' empty result. The app then parses fields that don't exist in the response (is_secure, security_score, security_grade, threats, should_use_vpn vs the actual id, network, risk_level, risk_score, security_issues, rogue_ap_detected, evil_twin_detected) — so WifiAuditResult is always defaults and provider._auditCurrentNetwork (network_provider.dart:306-341) can never add a threat. The genuinely good backend analysis logic is unreachable.
fix: Align the contract: change Dart WifiAuditRequest.toJson() to emit {current_network: {ssid, bssid, security_type, signal_level, is_hidden}, nearby_networks: [...]} including the platform scan results, and rewrite WifiAuditResult.fromJson to parse risk_level/risk_score/security_issues/rogue_ap_detected/evil_twin_detected/recommendations (objects, not strings). Add an integration test that posts the exact Flutter payload to the Go handler and asserts non-empty issues for an open network.

title: VPN connect/disconnect/status endpoints do not exist on backend; VPN tab is a fake feature
location: APP lib/services/api/orbguard_api_client.dart:1813-1842 (GET /api/v1/vpn/status, POST /vpn/connect, POST /vpn/disconnect) vs LAB internal/api/router.go:714-717
detail: The router only registers GET /vpn/servers, /vpn/blocked, /vpn/stats (OrbNet aliases). getVpnStatus/connectVpn/disconnectVpn all 404. NetworkProvider.refreshVpnStatus swallows the error into 'disconnected'; connectVpn surfaces a raw error. Even with routes, POSTing 'connect' to a threat-intel REST API can never establish a device VPN tunnel — there is no NEVPNManager/VpnService integration anywhere in this chain — so the VPN tab (network_security_screen.dart:220-305, hardcoded 6-server list at :682-690) presents a capability the product does not have.
fix: Either integrate a real client-side VPN (OrbVPN/OrbNet SDK: fetch servers from GET /vpn/servers, establish the tunnel on-device via platform VPN APIs, report local tunnel state) and keep the backend only for server lists/recommendations; or remove the VPN connect UI and replace it with a deep link to the OrbVPN app plus the existing /network/vpn/recommend endpoint. Delete the hardcoded server list and the dead /vpn/status|connect|disconnect client methods.

title: DNS protection enable/disable/status endpoints missing and no device-side DNS mechanism — DNS tab shows fake 'Protected' state
detail: Backend only has /network/dns/check|providers|configure — none of the three endpoints the app calls exist, so all 404. enableDnsProtection's error is stored but the bottom sheet closes as if it succeeded. More fundamentally, enabling 'DNS protection' via a remote REST call cannot change the device's DNS — no platform channel, no VpnService/NEDNSSettingsManager is invoked. Even the backend's own ConfigureDNS handler (handlers/network_security.go:124-145) literally says 'In production, this would configure the device's DNS. For now, just return the configuration.'
fix: Implement real DNS protection on-device: Android private-DNS guidance or a local VpnService-based DoH resolver, iOS NEDNSSettingsManager profile pointing at the OrbGuard filtering resolver; back it with backend routes GET /dns/status and POST /dns/enable|disable that persist per-device config (new dns_configs table) and return the actual resolver IPs. Until then, remove the 'Enable DNS Protection' action or mark it unavailable.

title: GET /network/threats has no backend route — Network Security threat feed silently empty
detail: getNetworkThreats() calls GET /api/v1/network/threats which is registered nowhere in router.go (only analytics /threats under a different prefix). The 404 is caught and debugPrint'ed in loadNetworkThreats, so init() always yields an empty threat list and the user is told there are no active network threats regardless of reality.
fix: Add a backend route GET /api/v1/network/threats returning persisted per-device threat records (requires storing AuditWiFi/ARP results keyed by device_id in Postgres — currently audits are computed and discarded), with fields id/type/title/description/severity/detected_at/is_active/recommendation matching the Dart parser; or drop the remote feed and derive threats purely client-side, removing the dead API call.

title: Rogue-AP REST routes are admitted placeholders aliased to wrong handlers; scan/trusted-list chain returns garbage
location: LAB internal/api/router.go:723-726 ('map to wifi/audit for now') vs APP lib/services/api/orbguard_api_client.dart:780-822 and lib/providers/rogue_ap_provider.dart:185-307
detail: POST /network/rogue-ap/scan is wired to AuditWiFi, but the app sends {access_points:[...]} which AuditWiFi decodes as an empty WiFiAuditRequest; the app then reads result['access_points'] which the WiFiAuditResult never contains, so detectedAPs is always []. GET /network/rogue-ap/trusted is wired to GetWiFiSecurityInfo which returns {security_types:[...]}, while the app reads 'trusted_aps' — always empty. POST /network/rogue-ap/trusted is wired to AuditWiFi, which persists nothing and returns no 'id', so 'Trust' only mutates client memory. DELETE /network/rogue-ap/trusted/{id} (api_config.dart:190, client:817-819) has NO route at all — removal 404s and the provider just debugPrints. The entire Rogue AP screen is a non-functional facade.
fix: Implement real handlers + persistence: a rogue_ap handler with POST /scan accepting {access_points:[...]} and returning analyzed APs (reuse NetworkSecurityService.detectRogueAPs/detectEvilTwin) with threat_level/threats per AP; a trusted_aps table with per-user GET/POST/DELETE CRUD. Remove the 'for now' aliases and add contract tests against the exact Flutter payloads.

title: Network Firewall feature is a complete facade — no native plugin, no event source, no enforcement
detail: No platform code anywhere registers these channels (grep across android/ios/macos/windows/linux finds nothing). initialize()/enable()/disable() throw MissingPluginException, swallowed by debugPrint. The event stream never emits, so the Live/Alerts/Apps tabs are permanently empty, and even if events existed, _checkConnection only labels them — nothing ever blocks a packet (no VpnService, no NEFilterDataProvider). The 1100-line firewall screen monitors and 'blocks' nothing.
fix: Implement real connection monitoring/blocking: Android VpnService-based local firewall (TUN interception, per-uid app attribution via ConnectivityManager.getConnectionOwnerUid, drop packets matching rules) emitting events on the EventChannel; iOS/macOS NEFilterDataProvider extension; or scope the feature to platforms where it's feasible and hide it elsewhere. Until then the screen must not ship as-is.

title: Firewall toggle reports 'Firewall Active' even when native enable failed
detail: NetworkFirewallService.enable() catches PlatformException and returns normally without setting its own _isEnabled. The provider then unconditionally sets _isEnabled = true after the await, so the status card (network_firewall_screen.dart:140-201) shows green 'Firewall Active / Monitoring network connections' when absolutely nothing is running — a false sense of security in a security product.
fix: Make service.enable() rethrow (or return bool) on failure, and have the provider set _isEnabled only from the service's authoritative isEnabled getter; surface the failure in the UI.

title: Firewall rules and block lists are in-memory only — lost on every app restart
detail: _rules, _blockedDomains, _blockedIps, _blockedCountries, _blockedApps are plain in-memory collections. addRule/removeRule/blockDomain/blockApp etc. never write to SharedPreferences, sqflite, or any backend endpoint (the Rules tab lets users create rules that silently vanish on restart). matchCount is also never incremented despite being displayed.
fix: Persist rules and block sets locally (sqflite or hive) with load on initialize(), and/or sync to the existing LAB desktop firewall-rule endpoints (POST/DELETE /api/v1/desktop/network/rules already exist in router.go:636-638). Increment matchCount in _checkConnection when a rule fires.

title: VPN blocklist parse mismatch: backend returns 'rules', client reads 'domains'
detail: getVpnBlockedDomains() (used by network_firewall_service.dart:300-311 to seed the malicious-domain blocklist) parses response.data['domains'], but GET /vpn/blocked -> ListBlockRules responds {rules: [...], count: n}. The cast yields [] every time and the failure is invisible (?? []), so the firewall's VPN-sourced blocklist is always empty.
fix: Read response.data['rules'] and map each rule's domain/pattern field (verify the BlockRule model's JSON tags), or add a 'domains' projection endpoint; add a decode test against the real handler output.

title: Rogue AP screen 'Block' button is a no-op
detail: On every threat card, the primary 'Block' action is `onPressed: () {}` — tapping it does literally nothing. A user who finds a dangerous AP and presses Block receives no protection and no feedback.
fix: Wire it to a real action: add the BSSID to a blocked list that triggers OS-level mitigation where possible (Android: suggest forget-network via settings intent + alert on reconnect; minimum: persist a block entry, alert when the device connects to that BSSID), or remove the button until blocking exists.

title: Rogue AP screen search bar is dead
detail: GlassTabPage is given hasSearch: true and searchHint 'Search networks...' but no onSearchChanged callback and no filtering of detectedAPs by query (contrast network_security_screen.dart:24-28 which implements it). Typing in the search field changes nothing.
fix: Add an onSearchChanged handler storing the query in state and filter sortedAPs/threats/trustedAPs by ssid/bssid before rendering, mirroring the implementation in network_security_screen.dart.

title: Rogue AP protection settings are decorative — never persisted, never enforced
detail: autoProtect ('Automatically enable VPN when threats detected'), alertOnRogue, and alertOnOpen are plain booleans toggled in memory: no SharedPreferences write, and no code anywhere reads them to auto-enable a VPN or fire a notification. The settings sheet promises behavior that has no implementation.
fix: Persist the three flags (SharedPreferences) and implement consumers: on scan results with dangerous APs, fire a local notification when alertOnRogue, and trigger the (real) VPN flow when autoProtect; or remove the settings sheet.

title: RogueAPDetectionService is dead code with no native channel and a stubbed trusted-network loader
detail: The 800-line service (with genuinely decent heuristics) is never instantiated by any provider or screen (only a comment in ios_forensic_service.dart:471 mentions it). Its channels 'com.orbguard/rogue_ap' and 'com.orbguard/wifi_events' have no native implementation, so scanNetworks() would throw immediately. _loadTrustedNetworks() is an empty stub: 'In production, load from secure storage. For now, start with empty'. Meanwhile RogueAPProvider duplicates a weaker version of this logic via the broken backend alias routes.
fix: Either wire this service in as the actual analysis engine for RogueAPProvider (running locally on platform scan results, fixing the channel name to the one implemented in finding #1, and persisting trusted networks via flutter_secure_storage), or delete it to avoid two divergent half-implementations.

title: ZeroDayProtectionService is never used anywhere; malicious-indicator sets never populated
location: APP lib/services/security/zero_day_protection_service.dart:235-265 (instantiated by no file in lib/)
detail: grep shows no import or construction of ZeroDayProtectionService outside its own file — the behavioral/anomaly engine, its streams, and analyzeAppBehavior/analyzeNetworkBehavior/analyzeURL are completely unreachable dead code. Additionally _knownMaliciousIPs and _knownMaliciousDomains (lines 244-245) are declared, never populated from the threat-intel API, and never consulted by any check.
fix: Integrate it: feed AppBehaviorMetrics from the app-security monitoring pipeline and NetworkBehaviorMetrics from the (to-be-built) firewall event stream, register a provider exposing onThreatAlert to the UI, and populate the malicious IP/domain sets from OrbGuardApiClient.listIndicators; otherwise remove the file.

title: Backend network GetStats returns zeroed placeholder data
detail: GetStats constructs an empty NetworkSecurityStats and returns it; the comment says 'In production, these would come from database. For now, return placeholder data' and the cache branch is an empty if-block. Any consumer of /network/stats receives all-zero metrics presented as real.
fix: Persist audit/ARP/SSL/rogue results (currently computed and discarded) into Postgres tables and aggregate counts here (total audits, attacks by type, hijacks detected, time-windowed), with Redis caching in the already-injected cache.

title: ConfigureDNS and UpdateVPNConfig handlers persist nothing while claiming success
detail: ConfigureDNS returns {status:'configured', message:'DNS configuration saved...'} without saving anything ('In production, this would configure the device's DNS'). GetVPNConfig returns a hardcoded VPNConfig literal, and UpdateVPNConfig echoes the request back with 'In production, this would save the configuration' — a subsequent GET returns the hardcoded defaults, not what the client PUT.
fix: Add device_configs persistence (device_id-keyed rows for DNSConfig and VPNConfig) in the repositories layer; GetVPNConfig should read the stored row (defaults only when absent) and UpdateVPNConfig/ConfigureDNS should upsert it.

title: Gateway-MAC change detection uses an unscoped in-memory map — cross-user false positives and no persistence
detail: knownGateways maps gateway IP -> MAC globally across ALL API clients with no device/user scoping. Nearly every home network uses 192.168.1.1/192.168.0.1, so the first user to check stores their router's MAC and every other user with the same gateway IP then triggers a CRITICAL 'Gateway MAC Address Changed — ARP spoofing' alert. The map is also process-memory only, so the baseline vanishes on every restart, and trustedNetworks (line 27) is written nowhere at all.
fix: Key the baseline by device_id (already present in ARPSpoofCheckRequest/WiFiAuditRequest) + gateway IP, persist it in Postgres/Redis with TTL, and only compare against that device's own history. Remove or wire the unused trustedNetworks map.

title: DNS hijacking/leak checks test the SERVER's resolver, not the client's network, with stubbed logic
detail: checkDNSHijacking calls net.DefaultResolver.LookupIP on the backend host — it can only ever detect hijacking of the API server's own DNS, never the mobile user's network, making the check meaningless for its purpose. expectedIP is hardcoded '' ('simplified - in reality would check against known good IPs'), isSuspiciousIP only flags private/loopback ('In production, this would check against threat intelligence'), and checkDNSLeaks explicitly says 'In production, this would [test]... For now, we'll just check if using encrypted DNS', flagging every unencrypted-DNS user as 'leak detected'.
fix: Move resolution to the client: have the app resolve canary domains through the network's configured DNS and POST the resolved IPs in DNSCheckRequest; backend compares against known-good IP sets / its threat-intel indicator DB. Replace the leak heuristic with a real leak-test (unique-subdomain canary against an OrbGuard authoritative zone logging which resolver IP queried it).

title: Hardcoded VPN server list in UI instead of the existing /vpn/servers endpoint
detail: _showVpnServerSelector presents a hardcoded 6-entry country list (US/UK/DE/JP/AU/CA) while the backend already exposes GET /api/v1/vpn/servers (router.go:715) and the client already has getVpnServers via ApiEndpoints.vpnServers. The shown servers bear no relation to real availability.
fix: Fetch the live server list from GET /vpn/servers and render it (with loading/error states) in the selector; delete the hardcoded tuple list. (Only meaningful once finding #3's real VPN integration exists.)

title: loadNetworkThreats and _checkForThreats clobber each other's threat lists
detail: Both methods start with _threats.clear(): a scan wipes API-loaded threats and audit-derived threats, and a threat refresh wipes locally detected evil-twin/insecure-network findings, so whichever runs last silently erases the other source. Stats (totalScans, threatsDetected) are also recomputed from this unstable list, and maliciousSitesBlocked is hardcoded 0 at line 571.
fix: Maintain separate collections (remoteThreats, localThreats) merged in the activeThreats getter, or clear only entries originating from the same source (tag threats with origin). Derive maliciousSitesBlocked from real block events once they exist.

title: App Security screen loads apps from a backend endpoint that does not exist
detail: AppSecurityProvider.loadApps() (lib/providers/app_security_provider.dart:190-232) fetches the device's installed apps from GET /api/v1/apps/installed. The LAB router registers only analyze, analyze/batch, reputation, sideloaded, privacy-report, trackers, permissions/dangerous, stats, report — no /installed. Every load 404s, the catch sets a generic error, and the Apps/Risks/Stats tabs are permanently empty ('No apps found'). The whole screen is dead. Conceptually the inventory should come from the device anyway: MainActivity.kt already implements a native getInstalledApps() (android/app/src/main/kotlin/com/orb/guard/MainActivity.kt:389-420) but the provider never uses it.
fix: Replace the API call in loadApps() with a platform-channel call to the existing native getInstalledApps() (com.orb.guard/system channel), mapping packageName/appName/version/permissions/installer into InstalledApp. If a server-side device app registry is genuinely wanted, add a GET /apps/installed handler + persistence in LAB; otherwise delete getInstalledApps() from the API client and api path.

title: POST /apps/analyze request contract mismatch — permissions sent as strings, backend expects objects (guaranteed 400)
detail: AppAnalysisRequest.toJson() sends permissions as a JSON array of strings (["android.permission.CAMERA", ...]). The Go handler decodes into []AppPermission (struct with name/is_granted/...), so json.Decode fails and AnalyzeApp (LAB internal/api/handlers/app_security.go:29-34) returns 400 'invalid request body' for every request. The 'Analyze Now' button and scanAllApps() therefore always fail. Additionally Flutter sends 'version' while Go expects 'version_name', and 'install_source' carries raw installer package ids ('com.android.vending') while Go's AppInstallSource enum expects 'play_store'/'sideloaded'/'adb'/'unknown' (app_security.go:24-30), so sideloaded detection in analyzeSecurityRisks (app_analyzer.go:320-322) can never trigger. Even if decoding succeeded, IsGranted is never sent, so GrantedDangerous is always 0 and the permission score is always 0.
fix: Align the contract: change AppAnalysisRequest.toJson() to emit permissions as [{name, is_granted: true, is_dangerous}] objects (or change Go to accept []string and classify server-side), rename version→version_name, and map installer package → enum (com.android.vending→play_store, com.apple.appstore→app_store, null/other→sideloaded/unknown) on the client before sending.

title: AnalyzeApp response contract mismatch — Flutter parses flat fields, backend returns nested structure
detail: Flutter expects top-level keys permission_risks (list), detected_trackers (list), is_sideloaded, is_known_malware, privacy_grade, warnings, recommendation (string). The backend returns nested permission_risk (object), privacy_risk.tracker_sdks, security_risk.is_sideloaded, threat_intel_match.is_known_malware, overall_verdict, and recommendations (array of objects) — and produces no privacy_grade or warnings at all. Result: even with a successful response, the app renders permissionRisks=[], detectedTrackers=[], isKnownMalware=false (malware alerts silently hidden!), privacyGrade='U' for every app, warnings empty, recommendation null. The entire detail bottom sheet and the Risks/Stats tabs show fabricated-empty data.
fix: Rewrite AppAnalysisResult.fromJson to parse the real backend shape (permission_risk.concerns/dangerous_combos → PermissionRisk list, privacy_risk.tracker_sdks → TrackerInfo list, security_risk.is_sideloaded, threat_intel_match.is_known_malware, overall_verdict → recommendation), and add a privacy_grade computation server-side (or derive grade from privacy_risk.score client-side).

title: Risk score scale mismatch: backend 0-100 vs provider thresholds 0-1
detail: AnalyzedApp.isHighRisk uses riskScore > 0.7 and isMediumRisk > 0.4, but the backend RiskScore is on a 0-100 scale (a single dangerous permission yields 5.0 x 0.30 = 1.5). Once analysis works, virtually every app would be classified 'High Risk', and RiskScoreGauge would also receive out-of-range values.
fix: Normalize: either divide the backend score by 100 when parsing, or change thresholds to >70 / >40 and update RiskScoreGauge/stat calculations to the 0-100 scale consistently.

title: Supply Chain scanning has no native implementation — MethodChannel 'com.orbguard/supply_chain' is unhandled on every platform
detail: No Kotlin/Swift code registers 'com.orbguard/supply_chain' (Android channels are 'com.orb.guard/system', '/sms', '/browser' — MainActivity.kt:31-33; iOS/macOS have nothing). Every scanApp() throws MissingPluginException; scanAllApps() catches it (line 770-773) and returns [], and SupplyChainProvider.scanAllApps (lib/providers/supply_chain_provider.dart:137-160) then reports 'Scan complete' with 0 apps. The Supply Chain screen's 'Scan Apps' button (lib/screens/supply_chain/supply_chain_screen.dart:51) is effectively a dead control that always produces an empty 'No Vulnerabilities / No Trackers / No Libraries' state presented as a clean result.
fix: Implement the native side: on Android add a 'com.orbguard/supply_chain' (or reuse com.orb.guard/system) handler with getInstalledPackages, getAppInfo, and getAppLibraries (enumerate DEX entries / META-INF or use PackageManager + dexlib to extract embedded package prefixes); on iOS return UnsupportedError gracefully. Also surface scan failures in the UI instead of returning [] silently.

title: All three /supply-chain backend endpoints used by the app do not exist
location: APP lib/services/api/api_config.dart:668-677 (supply-chain/vulnerabilities, /check, /trackers) vs LAB internal/api/router.go (no 'supply' routes anywhere)
detail: OrbGuardApiClient.getSupplyChainVulnerabilities (orbguard_api_client.dart:1889), checkSupplyChainLibraries (:1903), and getTrackerSignatures (:1918) call /api/v1/supply-chain/* which is registered nowhere in the LAB router and has no handler or service. SupplyChainMonitorService._loadVulnerabilityDatabase (:408-439) and _loadTrackerSignatures (:442-450) swallow the resulting 404s with debugPrint, so the CVE database and tracker-signature set are always empty — the 'CVEs' tab can never show a vulnerability even if scanning worked.
fix: Add a supply_chain handler + service in LAB: GET /supply-chain/vulnerabilities (CVE records per library, backed by an OSV/NVD-fed table), POST /supply-chain/check (batch library+version → matching CVEs with proper semver range evaluation), GET /supply-chain/trackers (tracker package prefixes, can reuse models.KnownTrackers). Register under api.Route("/supply-chain") in router.go, and make the Flutter loaders propagate failures to the provider instead of debugPrint.

title: GetAppReputation returns hardcoded 'safe' reputation for every package
detail: The handler ignores all data sources and returns a literal AppReputation{RiskLevel: Safe, RiskScore: 0, IsVerified: false} with the comment 'In production, this would query a reputation database'. Any client calling GET /apps/reputation/{package} for known malware gets 'safe'. (The Flutter client currently doesn't call it, limiting user impact, but the route is publicly exposed as real.)
fix: Back the route with the analyzer's threat-intel lookup (repos.Indicators.GetByValue on package name, as AnalyzeApp already does) plus a reputation table; or remove the route until implemented.

title: Backend tracker detection matches tracker SDK prefixes against the app's own package name — never detects embedded trackers
detail: analyzePrivacy does strings.Contains(req.PackageName, pkgPrefix) over models.KnownTrackers. An app's package name (e.g. com.instagram.android) never contains its embedded SDK prefixes (com.appsflyer, io.branch...), so TrackerSDKs is effectively always empty for real apps — tracker counts, privacy score contributions, and the 'High Tracker Count' recommendation are all silently inert. The request model has no field for the app's embedded libraries, so there is no data path for real detection.
fix: Add a detected_libraries []string field to AppAnalysisRequest, have the client extract embedded package prefixes natively (same native work as the supply-chain channel) and match those against KnownTrackers; until then, do not present tracker results as analyzed.

title: GetStats returns an empty placeholder struct
detail: GetStats (serving GET /apps/stats, router.go:224) returns a freshly allocated AppSecurityStats with empty maps and zero counts, with the comment 'In production, this would query the database'. Related: AnalyzeApp never persists results, so there is nothing to aggregate.
fix: Persist AppAnalysisResult rows (new app_analyses repo/table) in AnalyzeApp/AnalyzeBatch, then implement GetStats with aggregate queries (counts by risk level, install source, malware detections).

title: ReportApp accepts user malware reports but persists nothing
detail: POST /apps/report only writes a log line and replies 'Thank you for your report. It will be reviewed by our team.' No DB row, no queue, no review workflow — reports vanish when logs rotate, while the response asserts a review will happen.
fix: Add an app_reports table + repository, insert package_name/report_type/description/device_id with status='pending', and expose them in the admin/intelligence review flow.

title: AppVettingService fabricates permissions and trackers via _simulate* methods and is wired to nothing
detail: vetApk() does not parse AndroidManifest.xml ('would need binary XML parser in production', line 365) and instead injects a fixed fake permission set (_simulatePermissionExtraction, lines 601-633: CAMERA/READ_CONTACTS/FINE_LOCATION for every APK) and three fake trackers (_simulateTrackerDetection, lines 636-660: Firebase/Facebook/Google Ads), then computes a 'risk score' from this fabricated data. vetPackage() only string-matches malware family names against the package string ('Would query Play Store / App Store API', line 542). Additionally, no provider/screen references AppVettingService at all — it is dead code shipping fake analysis.
fix: Either implement it for real (binary AXML parser for manifest permissions, DEX package-prefix scan for trackers, cert chain extraction from META-INF) and wire it into an install-vetting flow, or delete the file. At minimum remove the _simulate* paths so it can never emit fabricated results.

title: AppUpdateMonitorService depends on a nonexistent native channel and hardcoded security scores, and is referenced by no UI
detail: Uses MethodChannel 'com.orbguard/app_update' with methods getInstalledApps and checkUpdates — neither the channel nor the methods exist in any native code (Android channels are com.orb.guard/*), so _loadAppHistory and checkForUpdates always hit the catch and debugPrint. VersionRecord.securityScore is hardcoded to 75.0 (lines 275, 637). No provider or screen imports this service, so the 'app update security monitoring' feature is entirely dead code.
fix: Implement the native channel (PackageManager-based installed list; update detection via versionCode diffing against stored history since Android exposes no pending-update API to third parties) and wire the service into a provider/screen, or remove it. Replace the hardcoded 75.0 with a computed score.

title: Supply chain CVE matching ignores affected version ranges
detail: _checkVulnerabilities attaches every CVE for a library prefix regardless of installed version ('In production, would check version ranges properly', line 632), guaranteeing false positives once the vuln DB loads. Similarly analyzeAppUpdate (lines 777-796) returns a stub map with recommendation 'Update analysis requires app store integration'.
fix: Implement semver/maven version-range evaluation against affectedVersions (or do matching server-side in the new /supply-chain/check endpoint which has the version data), and either implement or remove analyzeAppUpdate.

title: Supply chain scan progress is theatrical and critical-vulnerability alerts are swallowed
location: APP lib/providers/supply_chain_provider.dart:104-122 (fake staged progress with Future.delayed) and :80-83 (alert listener only debugPrints)
detail: scanApp() displays fixed progress stages (0.2 'Analyzing dependencies', 0.8 'Analyzing trackers') driven by Future.delayed timers rather than actual work, and the onVulnerabilityAlert listener's body is just debugPrint with the comment 'Handle critical vulnerability alert' — critical CVE alerts never reach the user.
fix: Drive progress from real per-app scan completion, and route vulnerability alerts to the app's notification/alert system (e.g. NotificationService or an in-app alert banner).

title: selectApp falls back to the first app when the package is not found
detail: selectApp uses firstWhere(..., orElse: () => _apps.first): if the package is missing it silently shows the wrong app's details, and if _apps is empty it throws StateError.
fix: Use _apps.where(...).firstOrNull and leave _selectedApp null (the detail sheet already guards on null).

title: All backend desktop security services are nil — every /desktop endpoint returns 503
detail: handlers.Dependencies has fields PersistenceScanner, CodeVerifier, NetworkMonitor, BrowserScanner, VTClient, but main.go never constructs any desktop_security service (no desktop_security.New* call exists anywhere in cmd/). All five are nil, so every handler in handlers/desktop_security.go hits its nil-guard and returns 503 'not available' — the entire 18-route /api/v1/desktop surface (router.go:627-645) is dead in the running binary.
fix: In cmd/api/main.go, construct desktop_security.NewPersistenceScanner(redisCache, log), NewCodeSigningVerifier(...), NewNetworkMonitor(...), NewBrowserExtensionScanner(...), and NewVirusTotalClient(cfg.VirusTotal.APIKey, redisCache, log) (add VT API key to config), and pass them into handlers.Dependencies. Add an integration test that asserts each /desktop route returns non-503.

title: App calls 3 desktop endpoints that have no backend route — initial load always fails
location: APP lib/services/api/api_config.dart:523-532 + lib/services/api/orbguard_api_client.dart:1568-1598 vs LAB internal/api/router.go:627-645; APP lib/screens/desktop/desktop_security_screen.dart:60-95
detail: ApiEndpoints.desktopPersistence (GET /api/v1/desktop/persistence), desktopApps (GET /desktop/apps), and desktopFirewall (GET /desktop/firewall) have no matching routes in router.go (backend only registers POST /desktop/persistence/scan etc.). _loadData() calls getPersistenceItems() first, gets a 404, and the catch sets _error — so the Desktop Security screen shows 'Failed to load desktop security data' on every launch, and _signedApps/_firewallRules are never populated. The Code Signing tab ('Running Applications') is permanently empty because no local data source exists for it either.
fix: Either add backend GET routes that return last-cached scan results (GET /desktop/persistence, GET /desktop/apps backed by a process-enumeration + codesign service, GET /desktop/firewall), or remove these three calls and drive the screen from the local provider on desktop (and add a local running-apps + code-signing enumerator in Dart for the Code Signing tab).

title: Backend desktop scanners scan the SERVER host, presented in the app as the user's device data
location: LAB internal/domain/services/desktop_security/scanner.go:53-75 (runtime.GOOS, server homeDir), network_monitor.go:96/361/424 (lsof/netstat on server), browser_extensions.go (server browser profiles); APP lib/screens/desktop/desktop_security_screen.dart:738-955 (Network and Browser tabs), 1455-1478 (mobile scanPersistence branch)
detail: PersistenceScanner, NetworkMonitor, and BrowserExtensionScanner all execute against the machine running the Go API (a Linux server), not the client's desktop. The app's Network tab ('Monitoring network activity') and Browser tab ('Extensions Found') would display the backend server's lsof output and browser-profile contents as if they were the user's, and the non-desktop _runScan branch would show the server's persistence items to a mobile user. This is architecturally fake data for an anti-spyware client.
fix: Decide the data flow explicitly: (a) do all collection client-side (extend the existing Dart scanners with network-connection and browser-extension collection via Process.run lsof/netstat and profile parsing — the persistence scanners prove this pattern works) and use the backend only for analysis/VT enrichment of uploaded results; or (b) repurpose the Go services as a self-hosted agent and accept POSTed scan payloads on the API. Remove the Network/Browser tabs' direct calls to server-local scan endpoints.

title: Firewall tab is cosmetic: hardcoded status, no-op switch, fake quick actions
detail: The Firewall status card always says 'Active and protecting your system' with a Switch(value: true, onChanged: (v) {}) that does nothing — no OS firewall state (socketfilterfw/netsh/ufw) is ever queried or set. The 'Block All Incoming', 'Stealth Mode', and 'App Permissions' quick actions show a confirmation dialog whose Enable button just pops a '$title enabled' success snackbar without executing anything. The per-rule enable Switch only flips local widget state.
fix: Query and control the real OS firewall via Process.run ('/usr/libexec/ApplicationFirewall/socketfilterfw --getglobalstate/--setglobalstate/--setstealthmode' on macOS, 'netsh advfirewall' on Windows, 'ufw'/'firewall-cmd' on Linux), reflect actual state, and wire quick actions to those commands (with privilege-escalation handling). Until implemented, remove or disable the controls instead of faking success.

title: Add Rule dialog only appends to a local in-memory list — never persisted or sent to backend
detail: _showAddRuleDialog's Add button does setState(() => _firewallRules.add(FirewallRule(...))) with hardcoded protocol 'TCP' and never calls _apiClient.addFirewallRule (which exists and matches backend POST /desktop/network/rules). Rules vanish on reload and never affect anything. deleteFirewallRule/getNetworkFirewallRules client methods are likewise never used.
fix: Wire the dialog to _apiClient.addFirewallRule (or to a local OS-firewall implementation per the previous finding), add port/address/protocol inputs, reload rules from the source of truth after add/delete, and surface failures.

title: Backend firewall rules and blocked IPs are in-memory only — no DB persistence, no OS enforcement
location: LAB internal/domain/services/desktop_security/network_monitor.go:479-525 (AddFirewallRule/RemoveFirewallRule/GetFirewallRules append/copy on a slice), 656-660 (BlockIP writes to map)
detail: AddFirewallRule appends to m.rules and BlockIP inserts into m.blockedIPs — pure in-process maps. Nothing is written to Postgres and nothing is applied to any actual firewall (no pf/iptables/netsh invocation). All rules and blocks are silently lost on restart; CheckConnection/markSuspicious only annotate scan output. The handler responds 'rule added'/'ip blocked' as if enforcement happened.
fix: Persist rules/blocked IPs to a repository table and, if enforcement is intended, apply them via the host firewall (or document/rename the API as advisory annotation only). Load persisted rules on startup.

title: JSON field contract mismatch: app parses fields the backend never emits (is_suspicious, remote_ip, is_running, auto_start, reason)
location: APP lib/screens/desktop/desktop_security_screen.dart:1967-1978 (PersistenceItem.fromJson) and 790-796 (network conn parsing) vs LAB internal/domain/models/desktop_security.go:103-150 (PersistenceItem: risk_level/code_signing, no is_suspicious/is_running/auto_start/reason) and 195-217 (NetworkConnection: remote_address, no is_suspicious field)
detail: Even with the backend wired, scanPersistence() results would render every item as 'Safe' (is_suspicious defaults false; backend sends risk_level instead) with no reason text, and network connections would show blank remote IPs (backend emits remote_address, app reads remote_ip/destination) and never flag suspicious connections (backend NetworkConnection has SuspicionReasons under different keys but no is_suspicious bool at the connection level the app reads).
fix: Align the DTOs: either add a serializer on the backend mapping risk_level>=high to is_suspicious, exposing reason and remote_ip, or update PersistenceItem.fromJson / the network tab parser to read risk_level, suspicion_reasons, and remote_address. Add a contract test deserializing a real backend response.

title: Known-good hash database is an empty placeholder
location: LAB internal/domain/services/desktop_security/scanner.go:758-762 (loadBuiltinHashes empty body, comment 'This is a placeholder for future known good hash database')
detail: KnownGoodDB.loadBuiltinHashes() is empty, so IsKnownGood() always returns false and the known-good allowlisting path in risk scoring is inert — every binary is treated as unknown, inflating noise.
fix: Seed the DB from a real source (e.g., NSRL RDS subset, or hashes of common signed system binaries per platform) loaded from a data file/DB table at startup, or remove the dead allowlist branch until implemented.

title: Registry quarantine is destructive: 'reg delete' without value backup, restore impossible
location: APP lib/providers/desktop_security_provider.dart:605-616 (_disableWindowsItem reg delete /f) and 770-775 (restoreItem: 'Registry items require manual restoration')
detail: Quarantining a Windows registry Run-key item deletes the value with reg delete /f without first exporting/recording the value data; metadata stores only name/path. restoreItem then refuses registry restores ('require manual restoration'), so a false-positive quarantine permanently destroys the user's startup entry with no undo — despite the UI advertising restorable quarantine.
fix: Before deletion, read the value data (reg query output is already parsed elsewhere in windows_persistence_scanner_service.dart) and store it in quarantine metadata (key path, value name, type, data); implement registry restore via 'reg add'. Also note reg delete on HKLM keys will silently fail without elevation — check exit codes (currently ignored) and report failure instead of returning true.

title: Network/Browser tab API errors silently swallowed — failures render as 'no data'
detail: getNetworkConnections() and scanBrowserExtensions() failures (currently guaranteed: 503 from nil services) are swallowed with catchError returning empty list/map, so the user sees 'No active connections detected' and 'No extensions data' instead of an error — a broken backend is indistinguishable from a clean result.
fix: Distinguish error from empty: keep a per-tab error state and show the existing error card pattern (as the Persistence tab does) when these calls throw, instead of returning empty sentinel values.

title: Quarantine action success is reported even when no branch handles the item type / commands fail
location: APP lib/providers/desktop_security_provider.dart:535-639 (_disableMacOSItem/_disableLinuxItem/_disableWindowsItem ignore Process.run exit codes; unmatched types return false with no _error set)
detail: All disable paths ignore Process.run exit codes (launchctl unload, systemctl disable, reg delete, schtasks, sc config can all fail on permissions) and return true regardless, so the UI shows 'has been quarantined' when nothing happened. For item types with no branch (e.g., macOS cron jobs, kernel extensions, Linux kernel modules — all advertised in 'Scan Coverage'), disableItem returns false with _error left null, producing 'Failed to quarantine X. ' with no reason.
fix: Check ProcessResult.exitCode on every command, propagate stderr into _error, and only report success on exit 0 + verified state change. Add disable handling (or an explicit 'not supported' message) for the remaining advertised persistence types.

title: Code signing, VirusTotal, quick-scan, listening/outbound backend endpoints have zero UI integration
location: APP lib/services/api/orbguard_api_client.dart:2056-2188 (quickScanPersistence, verifyCodeSigning, getListeningPorts, getOutboundConnections, getNetworkFirewallRules, addFirewallRule, deleteFirewallRule, blockIpAddress, vtLookupHash, vtLookupIp, fullDesktopScan) — no callers anywhere in lib/
detail: Eleven implemented client methods (and their backend counterparts: codesign/verify, codesign/verify-batch, virustotal/hash|file|batch|ip, scan/full, network/listening|outbound|rules, persistence/scan-path) are never invoked by any screen or provider. The substantial CodeSigningVerifier (490 lines) and VirusTotalClient (548 lines) backends are unreachable from the product, and the Code Signing tab that should consume them reads a nonexistent endpoint instead.
fix: Wire the Code Signing tab to verifyCodeSigning/verify-batch (feeding paths from the local persistence scan), add VT hash lookup to the persistence-item detail sheet (binary_hash is already computed client-side via shasum/sha256sum), or delete the dead surface to reduce maintenance.

title: FullSecurityScan handler swallows all sub-scan errors and returns partial result as success
detail: FullSecurityScan runs persistence/browser/network scans with 'if err == nil' guards that silently drop failures; a fully failed scan returns 200 with just {"scan_type":"full"} and no indication anything failed.
fix: Collect per-component errors into an errors array in the response (and return 207/500 when all components fail) so clients can distinguish a clean empty result from failure.

title: Four forensics endpoints 404: client URLs diverge from registered routes (and from its own api_config constants)
detail: Client calls GET /forensics/ioc/stats (route is /forensics/iocs/stats), POST /forensics/analyze/shutdown (route is /analyze/shutdown-log), POST /forensics/analyze/full (route is /full-analysis), POST /forensics/check (route is /quick-check). All four return 404 in production. Ironically api_config.dart:749,753,773,777 defines the correct paths (forensicsIocStats, forensicsAnalyzeShutdownLog, forensicsFullAnalysis, forensicsQuickCheck) but the client hand-concatenates '${ApiEndpoints.forensics}/...' instead of using them. Shutdown Log Analysis, Full Forensic Scan, Quick Check and IOC stats are all dead.
fix: In orbguard_api_client.dart replace the hand-built strings with the existing ApiEndpoints constants: ApiEndpoints.forensicsIocStats, ApiEndpoints.forensicsAnalyzeShutdownLog, ApiEndpoints.forensicsFullAnalysis, ApiEndpoints.forensicsQuickCheck. Add an integration test that asserts every client path exists in router.go.

title: IOCs tab always shows hardcoded fake statistics (2500 IOCs / 850 Pegasus / 320 Predator / 1100 Stalkerware / 230 other)
detail: Because getIOCStats() always 404s (wrong path, see above), loadIOCStats() always lands in the catch block, which silently swallows the error and substitutes invented numbers presented to the user as the live 'IOC Database' with 'Last Updated: today'. Even if the path were fixed, the real scanner only holds ~22 built-in IOCs plus 11 patterns (ioc_scanner.go), nowhere near 2500 — the UI fabricates a capability the backend does not have.
fix: Remove the fake-stats fallback; on error show an error/retry state instead. Fix the endpoint path, then make the backend stats real by wiring the IOC scanner to the threat-intel indicator store (see LoadFromIndicators finding) so totals reflect actual data.

title: Response contract mismatch: app parses 'findings' but backend returns 'anomalies'/'detected_threats' — every analysis renders 'No Threats Found'
detail: _runAnalysis() reads response['findings'] (objects with id/title/description/severity/category/indicators) and response['total_checked']. The backend ForensicResult JSON has no such keys — it emits 'anomalies' (id/title/description/severity/confidence/path/process_name), 'detected_threats', 'total_anomalies', 'infection_likelihood'. So for the endpoints that do reach the backend (backup, data-usage, sysdiagnose, logcat once body keys are fixed), the app will always display a green 'No Threats Found' result with 0 findings even when the backend detected Pegasus. Same for quickCheck: provider reads response['matches'] (forensics_provider.dart:268) but QuickCheckResult emits 'indicators' (service.go:496). For a spyware-detection product this is a silently-wrong-verdict bug.
fix: Map the real backend schema in the provider: build ForensicFinding objects from response['anomalies'] and response['detected_threats'] (title, description, severity, confidence, path/process as indicators, mitre_techniques/remediation in metadata), use total_anomalies and infection_likelihood. For quickCheck parse 'indicators'/'is_suspicious'. Alternatively add a translation layer in the Go handlers, but pick one contract and add a round-trip test.

title: Request body keys mismatch on every analyze endpoint (content/path vs log_data/backup_path/archive_path/db_path)
detail: App sends {'content': ...} for shutdown log and logcat; handlers require 'log_data' and return 400 'log_data is required' (logcat is the only endpoint whose path matches, and it still always fails). App sends {'path': ...} for backup and sysdiagnose; handlers decode 'backup_path'/'archive_path', get empty strings, and the parsers fail os.Stat/sql.Open → 500 'analysis failed'. analyzeDataUsage sends whatever map it gets ({} from the UI); handler wants 'db_path'. No device_id is ever sent either, so results can't be attributed to a device.
fix: Align the client payloads to the handler structs: {'device_id', 'log_data'} for shutdown-log/logcat, {'device_id', 'backup_path'} / {'archive_path'} / {'db_path'} for the path-based ones (after solving the upload-architecture finding). Add JSON-schema or shared-fixture contract tests between the two repos.

title: Backup, Sysdiagnose and Data Usage analysis are architecturally impossible: server stats its own filesystem for a path on the user's device
location: APP lib/screens/forensics/forensics_screen.dart:954-970,1042-1115 (path text-field sheets) -> LAB internal/api/handlers/forensics.go:122-185 -> internal/forensics/ios/backup_parser.go:96, ios/datausage.go:77, ios/sysdiagnose.go:95
detail: The UI asks the user to type '/path/to/file' for their iOS backup or sysdiagnose archive and POSTs that string to the remote API. The Go parsers then os.Stat/sql.Open that path on the API server's filesystem — which can never contain the user's phone backup. Upload endpoints exist only for shutdown.log and logcat (router.go:605-606), and even those are not used by the app. There is no upload, chunking, or client-side parsing path for Manifest.db, DataUsage.sqlite or sysdiagnose .tar.gz, so three of the six advertised analysis types cannot ever work for a real user.
fix: Add multipart/chunked upload endpoints (e.g. POST /forensics/ios/backup/upload accepting a zipped backup or just Manifest.db, /forensics/ios/sysdiagnose/upload for the .tar.gz, /forensics/ios/data-usage/upload for DataUsage.sqlite), store to a temp dir, run the existing parsers against the stored path, and replace the path-text-field sheets with a file picker + upload-with-progress flow. Alternatively parse Manifest.db/DataUsage.sqlite client-side (sqflite) and send extracted records.

title: 'Full Forensic Scan' button sends an empty request — nothing is ever scanned
detail: The flagship 'Full Forensic Scan / Comprehensive analysis for all known spyware' button posts {} (currently to a 404 path, /analyze/full). Even with the path fixed, RunFullAnalysis requires platform plus at least one of shutdown_log/logcat_data/backup_path/etc.; with an empty request every branch is skipped and a pristine empty result ('No Threats Found') is returned. The app collects no device artifacts (no logcat capture, no shutdown.log retrieval, no data usage snapshot) before calling it. This is the core feature of the screen returning a fake clean verdict.
fix: Fix the path to /forensics/full-analysis, then make the client gather real inputs before calling: platform from Platform.isIOS/isAndroid, logcat capture via a platform channel on Android, uploaded artifacts on iOS, and pass include_timeline. Disable or hide the button until at least one artifact source is available, instead of returning a clean result from nothing.

title: 'Data Usage Analysis' button posts an empty map — no data usage is ever collected from the device
detail: The button claims to 'Detect suspicious network activity patterns' but sends {} with no db_path and no usage data; the handler passes an empty path to DataUsageParser.Parse which fails to open the sqlite DB → 500 → user sees 'Analysis failed'. There is no code anywhere in the app that reads per-app network usage (iOS DataUsage.sqlite is not accessible without backup/sysdiagnose anyway).
fix: Either remove the button on platforms where it cannot work, or implement it properly: on Android collect NetworkStatsManager per-app usage and add a backend endpoint accepting structured usage records; on iOS feed it from an uploaded backup/sysdiagnose (the parser already reads netusage.sqlite from sysdiagnose archives).

title: IOSForensicService is a complete hollow stub and dead code — all 10 analyzers return empty lists
detail: _runQuickScan, _analyzeShutdownLog (its detection logic is literally commented out, lines 433-451), _checkRunningProcesses, _analyzeNetworkConnections, _analyzeDataUsage, _analyzeSysdiagnose, _analyzeInstalledApps, _analyzeConfigProfiles, _analyzeBackup, _checkCustomIOCs all return empty findings with 'In production, this would...' comments, while runScan() fabricates processedArtifacts (+10, +50, +200, +1000) and generateRemediationReport prints 'No Indicators of Compromise Found'. The class is not imported by any other file (provider talks to the backend directly), so it is 712 lines of dead, misleading code.
fix: Delete the file, or repurpose it as the artifact-collection layer the screen actually needs (stalkerware bundle-ID checks via installed-app queries on Android, backup file handling, custom IOC checks against the backend quick-check endpoint) and wire it into ForensicsProvider. Do not keep a scanner that always reports clean.

title: IOC scanner never loads real threat intel — LoadFromIndicators has zero callers; only ~22 hardcoded IOCs exist
location: LAB internal/forensics/ioc_scanner.go:309 (LoadFromIndicators, no callers), cmd/api/main.go:222 (NewServiceWithCache only)
detail: The scanner exposes LoadFromIndicators(ctx, indicators) to ingest the platform's threat-intelligence indicator store, but nothing ever calls it — the 'IOC database' used by all forensic scans is permanently the small built-in list (18 Pegasus domains, 4 Predator domains, 1 placeholder hash, 6 path patterns, 5 process patterns). The Redis cache passed into NewIOCScanner is also never used (iocCacheKeyPrefix/iocCacheTTL at lines 18-19 are dead constants). The app meanwhile advertises 'IOC database from Citizen Lab, Amnesty Tech MVT' with thousands of entries.
fix: On service startup (and on a refresh schedule), query the domain indicator repository for spyware-tagged indicators and call iocScanner.LoadFromIndicators; use the Redis cache for the loaded set with the already-defined TTL. Expose the resulting counts via GetIOCStats so the app shows real numbers.

title: Placeholder malicious-file hashes: hash IOC matching can never match anything real
location: LAB internal/forensics/ioc_scanner.go:276-298 ('a1b2c3d4e5f6...'.repeat pattern, comment 'Placeholder hashes') and internal/forensics/ios/backup_parser.go:367-376 (isKnownMaliciousHash with 'a1b2c3d4e5f6...': true)
detail: Both the IOC scanner's hash list and the backup parser's isKnownMaliciousHash contain admitted placeholder values ('Placeholder hashes - in production these would be loaded from threat intel feeds'). backup_parser.go even uses the truncated string 'a1b2c3d4e5f6...' which can never equal a 64-char SHA256. The backup scanner dutifully SHA256-hashes every file under 10MB (scanBackupFiles, lines 302-349) and then compares against an impossible set — wasted compute presented as 'Known Malicious File Hash Detected' capability.
fix: Replace both placeholder sets with hashes loaded from the threat-intel indicator store (IndicatorTypeHash) via the same LoadFromIndicators wiring; have BackupParser query the IOCScanner (inject it) instead of keeping its own private map. If no real hash feed exists yet, remove the hash-check code paths rather than shipping dead detection.

title: parseBplist is an empty stub — backup file sizes and modification timestamps are never extracted
detail: parseBplist returns an empty map (comment: 'In production, use a proper bplist parser like howett.net/plist. This is a simplified version'). Consequently every BackupFileInfo has zero Size and zero-value ModifiedAt, so backup anomalies and timeline events (lines 163, 176-192) all carry timestamp 0001-01-01 and size 0, destroying timeline reconstruction and the 'modified_at' evidence shown to users.
fix: Add github.com/DHowett/go-plist (howett.net/plist) and decode the Manifest.db 'file' BLOB (NSKeyedArchiver bplist) to extract LastModified, Size, Mode and UserID; populate BackupFileInfo accordingly and skip timeline events when no timestamp is available.

title: Forensic results are never persisted — History tab is RAM-only and there is no server-side record of analyses
location: APP lib/providers/forensics_provider.dart:116 (_analysisHistory in-memory list), LAB internal/api/handlers/forensics.go (no store; results only json-encoded to the response)
detail: Analysis results live only in the provider's in-memory list; restarting the app empties the History tab.
