detail: SetServingStatus("threatintel.v1.ThreatIntelligenceService", SERVING) advertises a healthy service that is never actually registered (finding 1), so load balancers / health probes would route traffic to a server that answers every RPC with 'unknown service'. Also the checker loop (lines 23-65) contains dead code: `case <-context.Background().Done()` (line 55) can never fire, and the select with `default:` (lines 52-58) is a no-op pass-through.
fix: Only mark the service SERVING after real registration succeeds. Simplify the loop to a time.Ticker(10s) with a cancellable context passed in from main (the current context.Background() ignores shutdown), and remove the impossible select cases.
domain: gRPC threat-intel server (LAB internal/grpc/threatintel + proto/threatintel/v1, wiring in cmd/api/main.go, consumers in orbnet/orbmesh/APP)

title: Server.aggregator dependency injected but never used
detail: The *services.Aggregator is stored on the Server struct but no method references s.aggregator (verified grep) — the GetThreatStats fallback path uses cache/repos instead. Dead dependency suggesting an intended aggregator-backed stats path was never finished.
fix: Either use the aggregator (e.g., GetThreatStats pulling live aggregation status / last-run info, which the REST handlers expose) or remove the field and constructor parameter.
domain: gRPC threat-intel server (LAB internal/grpc/threatintel + proto/threatintel/v1, wiring in cmd/api/main.go, consumers in orbnet/orbmesh/APP)

title: APIKeyAuth accepts ANY non-empty token — authentication is not enforced
detail: The validation block `if apiKey != secret && apiKey != "dev-api-key" { // For now, allow any non-empty key for development }` has an EMPTY body. Any caller supplying any non-empty Bearer token passes auth and reaches every /api/v1 protected route (intelligence, campaigns, actors, admin-adjacent data, device commands, etc.). The issued login/device tokens stored in Redis (auth:token:, auth:apikey:) are never consulted here, so the whole token system is bypassable. This protects nothing.
fix: Validate the presented key: look it up in Redis (cache.GetJSON for `auth:token:`+apiKey or `auth:apikey:`+apiKey) and reject (401) on miss. Set the resolved user_id/device_id into context. Remove the empty allow-all branch and the `dev-api-key` literal. Gate any dev shortcut behind cfg.App.Environment=="development" only.

title: Login issues tokens without verifying credentials
detail: Login decodes email/password, checks only that they are non-empty, then immediately issues a random 32-byte token + refresh token + random user_id. There is no lookup of the user, no password hash comparison, no account existence check. Any email/password pair returns valid Bearer tokens. Combined with the permissive middleware this is a full auth bypass.
fix: Authenticate against the real user store (orbnet user service / a users repository): fetch user by email, compare password with bcrypt against a stored hash, and only then issue tokens bound to the real user_id. Return 401 on mismatch. If this service is not meant to do password auth, remove the endpoint and rely on device api_key + upstream auth instead.

title: Hardcoded admin token 'dev-admin-token' grants admin in any environment
detail: AdminAuth accepts X-Admin-Token equal to the JWT secret OR the literal string "dev-admin-token". Anyone sending `X-Admin-Token: dev-admin-token` gains admin access to /api/v1/admin (force source updates, report approve/reject, detailed stats) regardless of environment.
fix: Remove the `dev-admin-token` literal. Validate admin via a real admin-role lookup (resolve the api key/user to a role flag, e.g. is_admin in DB) rather than a shared static token. If a static admin secret is unavoidable, require it to be a non-empty env-provided value and reject when unset, and never ship a default literal.

title: Issued auth tokens are stored but never validated — token lifecycle is non-functional
detail: Login/Refresh/RegisterDevice write tokens to Redis keys auth:token:, auth:refresh:, auth:apikey:. No code path ever reads auth:token: or auth:apikey: to validate an incoming request (grep confirms only writes exist). So token issuance, expiry (24h/30d TTL), and revocation are all cosmetic — they have zero effect on access control because the middleware ignores them.
fix: Wire APIKeyAuth (and a real JWT/opaque-token validator) to read these Redis keys, enforce expiry, and set identity into context. Implement Refresh to actually require the old token to exist (it does delete it — good) and have the middleware honor the new one.

title: APP never stores the access token after device registration (response key mismatch)
detail: RegisterDevice backend responds with {device_id, api_key, expires_at, message}. The Flutter client reads `data['token']` (always null) and only saves the token if non-null, so it never persists an access credential. AuthInterceptor.onRequest therefore adds no Authorization header, and every protected endpoint would 401 — except the broken middleware lets unauthenticated-but-tokened calls through, masking the bug. The intended auth (device api_key) is silently dropped.
fix: Read `data['api_key']` and call _authInterceptor.saveTokens(accessToken: apiKey). Align field naming across client and server (pick `api_key` or `token` consistently).

title: Token refresh permanently broken — reads 'access_token' but server returns 'token'
detail: _refreshAccessToken reads data['access_token'] and data['refresh_token'], but the backend Refresh response uses key 'token' (not 'access_token'). newAccessToken is always null, so refresh always fails, tokens get cleared, and the 401-retry recovery flow never works.
fix: Read `data['token']` (or standardize the backend to emit 'access_token'). Ensure both Login and Refresh responses and the client parser use identical keys.

title: Real third-party breach API keys hardcoded in client source
detail: Production API keys are committed in source and shipped in every app binary: HIBP `153d2cd2165c4841b7f7bcea1e89f702`, LeakCheck `0a81d813291df7d42c881f3495fb28cd1fa3becd`, IntelX `312e04bf-8fb0-4f09-b36e-34c8dc0c7a5a`. These are trivially extractable from the APK/IPA, can be abused/exhausted by anyone, and HIBP/IntelX keys are paid. They are also unrotatable without an app release.
fix: Remove keys from source. Proxy these breach lookups through the LAB backend (which already has HIBPConfig and a darkweb handler) so the keys live server-side in env/secrets. The client should call /api/v1/darkweb/check/email etc., never the third-party APIs directly. Rotate all three leaked keys.

title: Hardcoded secrets in deployment manifests
detail: kubernetes.yaml ships a base64 Secret whose value is literally `secret123`, used as the API key. The compose files set the JWT secret (which is the same value APIKeyAuth/AdminAuth compare against) to weak literals. Since JWT.Secret doubles as the valid api key and admin token, `secret123`/`dev-jwt-secret` become working production credentials.
fix: Inject JWT secret and API keys from a real secret manager (sealed secrets / vault / CI-provided env), never commit literals. Generate a high-entropy JWT secret per environment. Remove the example values from kubernetes.yaml and document required env vars instead.

title: CORS allows credentials together with localhost origins in shipped config
location: LAB config.yaml:56-76 (allowed_origins includes http://localhost:3000/8080, allow_credentials: true)
detail: The default config enables AllowCredentials=true while whitelisting http://localhost origins alongside production origins. If this config ships to production, it broadens the credentialed CORS surface (localhost dev tooling / malicious local apps can make credentialed cross-origin calls). The combination of credentialed CORS with a permissive auth layer compounds risk.
fix: Split CORS origins per environment: production should list only https://admin.orbai.world / https://api.orbai.world and drop localhost. Drive allowed_origins from env. Keep AllowCredentials only for the exact trusted origins.

title: Device registration drops OS/app metadata due to field-name mismatch
detail: Client sends keys `version`, `sdk_int`, `name` (Android/iOS). Backend RegisterDevice expects `os_version`, `app_version`, `device_name`. So os_version is stored empty, app_version is never sent at all, and device_name is lost. The persisted device row and cache get blank OS/version fields, degrading any auth/risk logic keyed on platform/OS.
fix: Align client payload to backend struct: send `os_version` (info.version.release / systemVersion), `device_name` (info.name), `app_version` (from package_info), and `model`/`manufacturer`. Or relax the backend to accept both. Add a shared schema/contract test.

title: Placeholder default API URL in threat intelligence client
detail: ThreatIntelligenceAPI defaults baseUrl to 'https://api.yourdomain.com/threat-intelligence' when apiUrl is null. main.dart currently passes a real URL, but the placeholder remains a latent footgun for any caller that constructs it without args, silently sending threat queries to a non-existent/unowned domain.
fix: Remove the placeholder default; make apiUrl required, or default it to ApiConfig.baseUrl. Fail fast if unset rather than pointing at yourdomain.com.

title: Dashboard protection score uses hardcoded feature flags
detail: GetProtection computes the user-visible protection score using locally hardcoded `smsEnabled/webEnabled/appEnabled/networkEnabled := true` and `vpnEnabled := false`, with a comment '// Feature flags (temporary hardcoded)'. The score is therefore identical for every device/user regardless of actual enabled protections, presenting fake personalization as real.
fix: Derive feature-enablement from the requesting device's real settings/state (device repository or per-account config) and compute the score from actual enabled modules, not constants.

title: LAB backend has zero Go tests in all 37 packages
location: LAB /Users/nima/Developments/orbguard.lab (repo-wide; `go test ./...` => '[no test files]' for every package)
detail: Not one *_test.go file exists in the entire threat-intel backend. Critical untested packages: internal/api/handlers (36 handler files, ~381 handler methods), internal/api/middleware (authentication/authorization), internal/domain/services (40 service files incl. ai, desktop_security, digital_footprint), internal/infrastructure/database/repository (all persistence), internal/detection/yara, internal/forensics/{android,ios}, internal/grpc/threatintel, and every feed ingester under internal/sources. Makefile:42-49 defines `test` and `test-coverage` targets that run against nothing — the test infrastructure is a facade.
fix: Add Go tests starting with the highest-risk paths: httptest-based handler tests for internal/api/handlers (auth, scan, threat-lookup endpoints), middleware auth tests, repository tests against a dockerized Postgres (sqlc), and table-driven unit tests for internal/domain/services scoring/detection logic. Wire `go test -race ./...` as a required gate before the deploy workflow.
domain: Build / Test Baseline (APP: orbguard Flutter, LAB: orbguard.lab Go)

title: APP has no test directory at all; `flutter test` fails outright
location: APP /Users/nima/Developments/orbguard (no test/ or integration_test/ dir; `flutter test` => 'Test directory "test" not found.')
detail: flutter_test is declared in dev_dependencies (pubspec.yaml:45-46) but there are zero test files. The ~160 Future-returning methods in lib/services/api/orbguard_api_client.dart, the 19 services in lib/services/security/, the ML classifiers (lib/services/ml/sms_classifier.dart, url_classifier.dart, behavior_analyzer.dart), the realtime/WebSocket stack, and all providers/screens are completely untested, so client-server contract drift with LAB cannot be detected automatically.
fix: Create test/ with: (1) unit tests for orbguard_api_client.dart using a mocked Dio/http adapter asserting paths, methods, payloads against api_config.dart; (2) unit tests for ML classifiers and security service scoring logic; (3) widget tests for dashboard/scan screens; (4) an integration_test/ suite that exercises the full screen->provider->API chain against a local LAB instance. Add `flutter test` to CI.
domain: Build / Test Baseline (APP: orbguard Flutter, LAB: orbguard.lab Go)

title: flutter analyze reports 1 ERROR: non-exhaustive switch on ConnectivityResult
detail: `networkTypeText` switches over ConnectivityResult? but does not handle ConnectivityResult.satellite (added in newer connectivity_plus). Analyzer classifies this as an error (non_exhaustive_switch_statement); total analyze output: 768 issues = 1 error, 70 warnings, 697 info.
fix: Add `case ConnectivityResult.satellite: return 'Satellite';` (or a default branch) to the switch at connection_manager.dart:229 and re-run flutter analyze to confirm 0 errors.
domain: Build / Test Baseline (APP: orbguard Flutter, LAB: orbguard.lab Go)

title: Deploy CI pipeline ships to production with no test/vet/lint gate
location: LAB .github/workflows/deploy.yml (only workflow in repo; APP has no .github at all)
detail: The single workflow checks out code, builds/pushes the Docker image, and deploys to Azure Container Apps — there is no `go test`, `go vet`, or lint step before deployment, so any regression goes straight to production. APP has no CI whatsoever.
fix: Add a `test` job (go vet ./..., go test -race ./..., golangci-lint) to deploy.yml and make the deploy job `needs: test`. Create a CI workflow for APP running `flutter analyze --fatal-warnings` and `flutter test`.
domain: Build / Test Baseline (APP: orbguard Flutter, LAB: orbguard.lab Go)

title: Empty cmd/worker binary entrypoint (dead/stub package)
detail: cmd/worker exists alongside cmd/api and cmd/aggregator but is completely empty — an apparently planned background-worker binary that was never implemented. Anything (Dockerfiles, docs, deploy assumptions) referencing a worker process has no implementation behind it.
fix: Either implement the worker main.go (consume from internal/streaming / brokers as intended) or delete the directory and remove any references so the repo reflects reality.
domain: Build / Test Baseline (APP: orbguard Flutter, LAB: orbguard.lab Go)

title: 70 analyzer warnings, many indicating declared-but-never-used detection logic
location: APP repo-wide (e.g. lib/services/security/ios_forensic_service.dart:219-235 (_pegasusFileIndicators/_pegasusDomains/_suspiciousProcessPaths unused), lib/services/security/zero_day_protection_service.dart:244-245 (_knownMaliciousIPs/_knownMaliciousDomains unused), lib/main.dart:540-636 (_scanNetwork/_scanProcesses/_scanFileSystem/_scanMemory/_addThreats/_matchCloudIntelligence unreferenced), lib/services/security/app_vetting_service.dart:252,289)
detail: Beyond cosmetic unused imports, a cluster of warnings shows core IOC datasets and scan-phase functions are declared but never invoked — i.e. the analyzer itself is flagging unwired detection paths (Pegasus indicators, malicious IP/domain lists, scan phases). These are functional-completeness red flags, not style issues, and overlap with other auditors' domains.
fix: Triage each unused_field/unused_element warning: wire the IOC lists and scan-phase functions into the active detection flow where they were intended, or delete confirmed-dead legacy code. Then enforce `flutter analyze --fatal-warnings` in CI to keep the count at zero.
domain: Build / Test Baseline (APP: orbguard Flutter, LAB: orbguard.lab Go)

title: Only 'test' artifact in LAB is an ad-hoc manual binary, not an automated test
detail: cmd/test-safebrowsing is a 4.5KB manual smoke-test executable for the Safe Browsing integration; it is not run by `go test` or CI and gives no regression protection. Similarly, APP tools/ contains only standalone Python forensic scripts (android_forensic.py, ios_forensic.py, network_monitor.py) outside any test harness.
fix: Convert the safe-browsing smoke check into a proper Go test (with an httptest mock of the Google Safe Browsing API plus an opt-in live-mode flag) so it runs under `go test ./...` and CI.
domain: Build / Test Baseline (APP: orbguard Flutter, LAB: orbguard.lab Go)
